hypothesis: A convolutional neural network (CNN) model trained on high-resolution retinal fundus images predicts diabetic retinopathy severity better than a traditional grading system based on expert annotations, using the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) as the primary metric.
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: CNN for Diabetic Retinopathy Severity Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling
This experiment uses a multi-center cohort of high-resolution retinal fundus images collected over five years from at least three geographically and demographically distinct clinical centers. The dataset comprises approximately 5,000 images, distributed across four severity grades according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) scale: no retinopathy (~3,250 images), mild (~1,000), moderate (~500), and severe (~250). This distribution reflects population prevalence and ensures the model trains on realistic class proportions.

Labels are assigned through a consensus grading process: two independent certified ophthalmologists grade each image independently using the ETDRS standard. Consensus is defined as agreement between ≥2 of 3 raters; images with initial disagreement are adjudicated by a senior ophthalmologist. This multi-rater approach mitigates subjective grading variability, which is a known confounder. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) is computed and reported alongside results.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy
Data is split via stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID, to ensure class distribution balance across train, validation, and test sets. This approach allows us to maintain the representative 3:1 class ratio even if changes in patient activity occur throughout the year. The split allocation is: 70% training (n≈3,500 images), 15% validation (n≈750 images), 15% test (n≈750 images). Stratification guards against the confounder of equipment and demographic variation; sensitivity analysis later evaluates whether the model generalizes across centers.

### 3. Image Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
All image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation. These transformations are then consistently applied to the training, validation, and test sets. Preprocessing steps are: (1) Resize to 512×512 to maintain clinical detail while ensuring computational efficiency; (2) Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]; (3) Per-channel standardization. Training augmentation includes mild rotation (±15°), flips, brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%), and elastic deformation to improve robustness. Validation and test images undergo no augmentation, reflecting deployment conditions. No hand-crafted features are engineered; the CNN learns end-to-end representations from raw pixel intensities.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The model is a standard deep CNN (e.g., EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50) pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on the retinal fundus dataset. The architecture includes global average pooling and a softmax layer for 4-class classification.

The baseline is the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally. To ensure fair comparison, the baseline is evaluated on the same stratified test set using identical metrics. The baseline represents the decision-maker the model would replace or augment in clinical practice; this is the correct counterfactual.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is macro-averaged AUROC across all four severity grades (one-vs-rest approach). AUROC is justified because: (1) it is threshold-independent, enabling flexible operational deployment; (2) it accounts for the asymmetric cost of errors (missing retinopathy is costlier than unnecessary referrals); (3) it is robust to class imbalance. Secondary metrics include per-class F1-score (to identify if the model fails on minority classes), weighted F1-score, sensitivity/specificity by grade (for clinical interpretability), and calibration plots (to assess reliability of predicted probabilities).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set, with early stopping based on validation AUROC. The test set is held out and used only once, at the very end, to report final performance. No test set information informs any tuning decision.

### 7. Controls and Sensitivity Analysis
Inter-rater reliability is computed to quantify label noise. Stratification by center ensures demographic and equipment diversity across folds. A sensitivity analysis trains separate models for each center and tests both within-center and cross-center, to assess generalization. Patient demographics are tracked and reported by fold to verify balance. The experiment establishes whether the CNN outperforms consensus expert grading on this specific multi-center cohort; claims are scoped to exclude populations or equipment not represented in the data.

planted_issues.description: Splitting data on a weekly basis instead of by patient ID introduces temporal leakage, as multiple images from the same patient could appear in both training and test sets. This inflates model performance by allowing the model to effectively 'see' parts of the patient during training that are later evaluated during testing, violating the independence assumption and compromising generalization validity.

Fitting preprocessing transformations (such as per-channel normalization statistics) on the full dataset leaks information from validation and test sets into the training pipeline. This can subtly bias the model toward better performance than would be achievable on truly unseen data, undermining the reliability of reported metrics.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline is not a fair comparator because it uses the same expert consensus grading used to label the data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This is a legitimate design choice. The baseline represents the operational system the model would replace. Comparing the model’s predictions to the same expert consensus used in labeling ensures a realistic evaluation of whether the CNN adds value over current clinical practice.

must_not_claim.claim: Per-channel standardization is inappropriate for medical images.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Per-channel standardization is a standard and effective preprocessing step in CNNs for image data and is properly justified here. The problem lies not in the technique itself but in when and how the normalization parameters are computed.

must_not_claim.claim: The class imbalance in the dataset (e.g., more 'no retinopathy' cases) invalidates the use of AUROC.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUROC is robust to class imbalance and is threshold-independent, making it suitable for this operational context where early detection of any retinopathy is critical. The design intentionally reflects real-world prevalence to ensure external validity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies both the temporal leakage from weekly splitting and the preprocessing leakage from fitting on the full dataset. The defender fails to rebut either, leading to a verdict that the experiment is invalid due to data leakage in both splitting and preprocessing.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly explains that patient-level independence is violated by weekly splits, and that fitting preprocessing on the full dataset leaks test information into training.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies one flaw (e.g., preprocessing leakage) and provides sufficient technical justification. The defender attempts to justify full-data fitting as 'ensuring stable estimates' but fails to acknowledge the statistical invalidity. The critic stands by their critique, which is correct.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic clearly explains that preprocessing must be fit only on training data to avoid leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender successfully argues that normalization was fit only on training data (not applicable here, as it contradicts the corrupted design)

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises both issues and the defender attempts to downplay them as minor. The critic demonstrates that both flaws systematically bias performance upward and undermine clinical validity. Final verdict: critique holds.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic shows that temporal leakage enables overfitting to patient-specific features and preprocessing leakage introduces subtle bias, both invalidating the reported performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender provides evidence that a patient-level split was actually used and preprocessing parameters were fit only on training data (which they were not, in this case)

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: CNN for Diabetic Retinopathy Severity Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling
This experiment uses a multi-center cohort of high-resolution retinal fundus images collected over five years from at least three geographically and demographically distinct clinical centers. The dataset comprises approximately 5,000 images, distributed across four severity grades according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) scale: no retinopathy (~3,250 images), mild (~1,000), moderate (~500), and severe (~250). This distribution reflects population prevalence and ensures the model trains on realistic class proportions.

Labels are assigned through a consensus grading process: two independent certified ophthalmologists grade each image independently using the ETDRS standard. Consensus is defined as agreement between ≥2 of 3 raters; images with initial disagreement are adjudicated by a senior ophthalmologist. This multi-rater approach mitigates subjective grading variability, which is a known confounder. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) is computed and reported alongside results.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy
Data is split at the patient level to prevent leakage: all images from a single patient appear in exactly one fold. Stratification occurs on both severity grade and clinical center, ensuring each fold preserves class distribution and center diversity. The split allocation is: 70% training (n≈3,500 images), 15% validation (n≈750 images), 15% test (n≈750 images). Stratification-by-center guards against the confounder of equipment and demographic variation; sensitivity analysis later evaluates whether the model generalizes across centers.

### 3. Image Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
All image preprocessing transformations are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Preprocessing steps are: (1) Resize to 512×512 to maintain clinical detail while ensuring computational efficiency; (2) Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]; (3) Per-channel standardization using training set mean and standard deviation. Training augmentation includes mild rotation (±15°), flips, brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%), and elastic deformation to improve robustness. Validation and test images undergo no augmentation, reflecting deployment conditions. No hand-crafted features are engineered; the CNN learns end-to-end representations from raw pixel intensities.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The model is a standard deep CNN (e.g., EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50) pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on the retinal fundus dataset. The architecture includes global average pooling and a softmax layer for 4-class classification.

The baseline is the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally. To ensure fair comparison, the baseline is evaluated on the same stratified test set using identical metrics. The baseline represents the decision-maker the model would replace or augment in clinical practice; this is the correct counterfactual.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is macro-averaged AUROC across all four severity grades (one-vs-rest approach). AUROC is justified because: (1) it is threshold-independent, enabling flexible operational deployment; (2) it accounts for the asymmetric cost of errors (missing retinopathy is costlier than unnecessary referrals); (3) it is robust to class imbalance. Secondary metrics include per-class F1-score (to identify if the model fails on minority classes), weighted F1-score, sensitivity/specificity by grade (for clinical interpretability), and calibration plots (to assess reliability of predicted probabilities).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set, with early stopping based on validation AUROC. The test set is held out and used only once, at the very end, to report final performance. No test set information informs any tuning decision.

### 7. Controls and Sensitivity Analysis
Inter-rater reliability is computed to quantify label noise. Stratification by center ensures demographic and equipment diversity across folds. A sensitivity analysis trains separate models for each center and tests both within-center and cross-center, to assess generalization. Patient demographics are tracked and reported by fold to verify balance. The experiment establishes whether the CNN outperforms consensus expert grading on this specific multi-center cohort; claims are scoped to exclude populations or equipment not represented in the data.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are subtle and masked by seemingly reasonable justifications (e.g., 'stable estimation', 'maintaining class ratios'), and both flaws must be identified for full credit. Domain-specific knowledge of patient-level data independence and correct preprocessing fit timing is required to detect the issues.
